question: 4. If \( f \) and \( g \) are real-valued functions of one real variable, show that there exist numbers \( x \) and \( y \) such that \( 0 \leq x \leq 1,0 \leq y \leq 1 \), and \( \mid x y-f(x)- \) \( g(y) \mid \geq 1 / 4 \)
solution:
Solution. Since
\[
1=(1-f(1)-g(1))+(f(1)+g(0))+(f(0)+g(1))-(f(0)+g(0))
\]
one of the numbers
\[
|1-f(1)-g(1)|,|f(1)+g(0)|,|f(0)+g(1)|,|f(0)+g(0)|
\]
is at least \( \frac{1}{4} \). Thus relation (1) holds for at least one of the points \( (1,1) \), \( (1,0),(0,1) \), or \( (0,0) \).

descriptive_long.question: 4. If \( functionf \) and \( functiong \) are real-valued functions of one real variable, show that there exist numbers \( variablex \) and \( variabley \) such that \( 0 \leq variablex \leq 1,0 \leq variabley \leq 1 \), and \( \mid variablex variabley-functionf(variablex)- \) \( functiong(variabley) \mid \geq 1 / 4 \)
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Since
\[
1=(1-functionf(1)-functiong(1))+(functionf(1)+functiong(0))+(functionf(0)+functiong(1))-(functionf(0)+functiong(0))
\]
one of the numbers
\[
|1-functionf(1)-functiong(1)|,|functionf(1)+functiong(0)|,|functionf(0)+functiong(1)|,|functionf(0)+functiong(0)|
\]
is at least \( \frac{1}{4} \). Thus relation (1) holds for at least one of the points \( (1,1) \), \( (1,0),(0,1) \), or \( (0,0) \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 4. If \( compassrose \) and \( mothership \) are real-valued functions of one real variable, show that there exist numbers \( shoreline \) and \( pinecones \) such that \( 0 \leq shoreline \leq 1,0 \leq pinecones \leq 1 \), and \( \mid shoreline pinecones-compassrose(shoreline)- \) \( mothership(pinecones) \mid \geq 1 / 4 \)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Since
\[
1=(1-compassrose(1)-mothership(1))+(compassrose(1)+mothership(0))+(compassrose(0)+mothership(1))-(compassrose(0)+mothership(0))
\]
one of the numbers
\[
|1-compassrose(1)-mothership(1)|,|compassrose(1)+mothership(0)|,|compassrose(0)+mothership(1)|,|compassrose(0)+mothership(0)|
\]
is at least \( \frac{1}{4} \). Thus relation (1) holds for at least one of the points \( (1,1) \), \( (1,0),(0,1) \), or \( (0,0) \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 4. If \( constant \) and \( unchanging \) are real-valued functions of one real variable, show that there exist numbers \( fixedvalue \) and \( certainvalue \) such that \( 0 \leq fixedvalue \leq 1,0 \leq certainvalue \leq 1 \), and \( \mid fixedvalue\,certainvalue-constant(fixedvalue)- \) \( unchanging(certainvalue) \mid \geq 1 / 4 \)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Since
\[
1=(1-constant(1)-unchanging(1))+(constant(1)+unchanging(0))+(constant(0)+unchanging(1))-(constant(0)+unchanging(0))
\]
one of the numbers
\[
|1-constant(1)-unchanging(1)|,|constant(1)+unchanging(0)|,|constant(0)+unchanging(1)|,|constant(0)+unchanging(0)|
\]
is at least \( \frac{1}{4} \). Thus relation (1) holds for at least one of the points \( (1,1) \), \( (1,0),(0,1) \), or \( (0,0) \).

garbled_string.question: 4. If \( qzxwvtnp \) and \( hjgrksla \) are real-valued functions of one real variable, show that there exist numbers \( vmbtcqzj \) and \( pwslnekr \) such that \( 0 \leq vmbtcqzj \leq 1,0 \leq pwslnekr \leq 1 \), and \( \mid vmbtcqzj pwslnekr-qzxwvtnp(vmbtcqzj)- \) \( hjgrksla(pwslnekr) \mid \geq 1 / 4 \)
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Since
\[
1=(1-qzxwvtnp(1)-hjgrksla(1))+(qzxwvtnp(1)+hjgrksla(0))+(qzxwvtnp(0)+hjgrksla(1))-(qzxwvtnp(0)+hjgrksla(0))
\]
one of the numbers
\[
|1-qzxwvtnp(1)-hjgrksla(1)|,|qzxwvtnp(1)+hjgrksla(0)|,|qzxwvtnp(0)+hjgrksla(1)|,|qzxwvtnp(0)+hjgrksla(0)|
\]
is at least \( \frac{1}{4} \). Thus relation (1) holds for at least one of the points \( (1,1) \), \( (1,0),(0,1) \), or \( (0,0) \).

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer n \geq  2 and put  

  P(x_1,\ldots ,x_n):=x_1x_2\cdots x_n    ((x_1,\ldots ,x_n)\in [0,1]^n).

For real-valued functions g_1,\ldots ,g_n on [0,1] write  

  \|P-(g_1+\cdots +g_n)\|\infty  := sup{ |P(x_1,\ldots ,x_n)-g_1(x_1)-\cdots -g_n(x_n)| : (x_1,\ldots ,x_n)\in [0,1]^n }.

1.  (A universal lower bound)  
    Show that for every n \geq  2 and every n-tuple of real functions f_1,\ldots ,f_n on [0,1] there exists (x_1,\ldots ,x_n)\in [0,1]^n such that  

  |P(x_1,\ldots ,x_n) - (f_1(x_1)+\cdots +f_n(x_n))| \geq  \frac{1}{4}.                                              (\star )

2.  (Two variables - how far can one get?)  
    Define  

  C_2 := inf_{g_1,g_2} \|P-(g_1+g_2)\|\infty    (with P(x,y)=xy).

    (a) Prove the lower bound \frac{1}{4} \leq  C_2 and the upper bound C_2 \leq  \frac{1}{3}.  
    (b) Consider the one-parameter linear family  

   g_1(x)=\alpha x, g_2(y)=\alpha y  (0\leq \alpha \leq 1).

        Show that within this family the best constant equals \frac{1}{3} and is attained at \alpha =\frac{1}{3}.  
        (Thus C_2 = \frac{1}{3} would follow if one could show that no essentially
        non-linear choice of (g_1,g_2) beats the linear ansatz - an open
        question that is left to the reader.)

3.  (Higher dimensions - quantitative bounds that are unconditional)  
    For n \geq  3 set  

   C_n := inf_{g_1,\ldots ,g_n} \|P-(g_1+\cdots +g_n)\|\infty .

    (a)  Prove the bounds  

    2^{-n} \leq  C_n \leq  \frac{1}{2}.  

    (b)  Let \theta \in [0,1/n].  Show that the symmetric linear choice  

    g_i(x)=\theta x  (1\leq i\leq n)

        satisfies  

    \|P-(g_1+\cdots +g_n)\|\infty  = max{ |1-n\theta | , (n-1)\theta  }.                   ()

        Deduce that \theta =1/(2n) gives the uniform error \frac{1}{2}, and that the
        optimal \theta  inside this linear family equals  

    \theta *_n = 1/(2n-1),

        for which the error in () equals  

    E*_n = (n-1)/(2n-1) < \frac{1}{2},

        and E*_n \nearrow  \frac{1}{2} as n\to \infty .

    (c)  Conclude that  

    limsup_{n\to \infty } C_n \leq  \frac{1}{2},

        whereas Part 3(a) gives  

    liminf_{n\to \infty } C_n \geq  0.

    Determining the exact limit (or even whether the limit exists) is a
    currently open problem.

(The three parts are independent.  In particular, one may solve 1 and 2
without touching 3.)
kernel_variant.solution:
Notation.  Throughout we abbreviate  

  \Sigma g(x_1,\ldots ,x_n) := g_1(x_1)+\cdots +g_n(x_n)  and  V_n := {0,1}^n.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Proof of the universal \frac{1}{4}-gap  

Fix any two coordinates, say the first two, and keep the remaining
coordinates equal to 1.  Put  

  F(t):=f_1(t), G(s):=f_2(s)+\sum _{k=3}^{n}f_k(1)   (0\leq s,t\leq 1).

The classical Putnam result (1963) asserts that there are x_1,x_2\in [0,1] with  

  |x_1x_2 - F(x_1) - G(x_2)| \geq  \frac{1}{4}.                                 (1)

Putting x_3=\cdots =x_n=1 makes P(x_1,\ldots ,x_n)=x_1x_2, and (1) is
precisely (\star ).  Hence Part 1 is proved.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The planar constant - sharp bounds and the best linear ansatz  

(a) Lower bound C_2 \geq  \frac{1}{4}.  

Let g_1,g_2 be arbitrary and write  

  a=g_1(0), b=g_1(1), c=g_2(0), d=g_2(1), \varepsilon :=\|P-(g_1+g_2)\|\infty .

Evaluating the approximation at the four vertices gives  

  |a+c|\leq \varepsilon ,     |b+c|\leq \varepsilon ,     |a+d|\leq \varepsilon ,     |1-b-d|\leq \varepsilon .                (2)

Subtracting the first two inequalities yields |a-b|\leq 2\varepsilon , and
subtracting the first from the third yields |c-d|\leq 2\varepsilon .
Now choose the three vertices (1,1), (1,0), (0,1); from (2) we get  

  |1-b-d|\leq \varepsilon , |b+c|\leq \varepsilon , |a+d|\leq \varepsilon .

Adding these three inequalities and invoking the triangle inequality
gives  

  |1-b-d|+|b+c|+|a+d| \geq  |(1-b-d)+(b+c)+(a+d)| = |1+a+c|.      (3)

Using |a+c|\leq \varepsilon  we have |1+a+c| \geq  1-\varepsilon , hence from (3)  

  1-\varepsilon  \leq  3\varepsilon   \Rightarrow   \varepsilon  \geq  \frac{1}{4}.                                   (4)

Because g_1,g_2 were arbitrary, C_2 \geq  \frac{1}{4}.

(b) Upper bound C_2 \leq  \frac{1}{3} and optimality inside the linear family.

For \alpha \in [0,1] put g_1(x)=\alpha x, g_2(y)=\alpha y and  

  E_\alpha (x,y):=|xy-\alpha (x+y)|.

Because E_\alpha  is bilinear/linear in each variable, its maximum on the unit
square is attained at a vertex; a short inspection shows  

  \|E_\alpha \|\infty  = max{\alpha , |1-2\alpha |}.                                    (5)

The right-hand side is minimised at \alpha =\frac{1}{3} and equals \frac{1}{3} there; whence
C_2 \leq  \frac{1}{3}.  Moreover, by (5) any choice \alpha \neq \frac{1}{3} produces an error
strictly larger than \frac{1}{3}, so \frac{1}{3} is the best possible **within the linear
one-parameter family**.  Whether some genuinely non-linear pair
(g_1,g_2) can beat \frac{1}{3} is an interesting open question; so far no
example is known, and \frac{1}{4} \leq  C_2 \leq  \frac{1}{3} is the best unconditional result.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Higher dimensions  

(a) General bounds 2^{-n} \leq  C_n \leq  \frac{1}{2}.

*  Lower bound.  
   For x\in V_n put \chi (x):=(-1)^{x_1+\cdots +x_n}.  Because P(x)=1 if x=(1,\ldots ,1) and
   P(x)=0 otherwise, one has  

  \sum _{x\in V_n} \chi (x)P(x) = \chi (1,\ldots ,1)=(-1)^n.                        (6)

   On the other hand,  

  \sum _{x\in V_n} \chi (x)\Sigma g(x)=
   \sum _{i=1}^{n} (g_i(0)-g_i(1)) \sum _{x\in V_n\{i}} \chi (x)=0,         (7)

   because the inner sum vanishes (there are equally many \pm 1 terms).
   Combining (6) and (7) and bounding every single error by
   E:=\|P-\Sigma g\|\infty  gives  

  1 = |\sum _{x\in V_n} \chi (x)(P-\Sigma g)(x)| \leq  2^n E,

   hence C_n \geq  2^{-n}.

*  Upper bound.  
   Choosing the linear ansatz g_i(x)=x/(2n) gives \Sigma g(x)=
   (x_1+\cdots +x_n)/(2n).  Because P(x)\leq min{x_1,\ldots ,x_n}\leq (x_1+\cdots +x_n)/n, we have  

  0 \leq  P(x)-\Sigma g(x) \leq  (1/n-1/(2n))(x_1+\cdots +x_n) \leq  \frac{1}{2},

   and clearly |P(x)-\Sigma g(x)|\leq \frac{1}{2} when P(x)\leq \Sigma g(x).  Thus C_n \leq  \frac{1}{2}.

(b)  The symmetric linear family g_i(x)=\theta x, 0\leq \theta \leq 1/n.

For such a choice, put  

  F(x_1,\ldots ,x_n)=x_1\cdots x_n - \theta (x_1+\cdots +x_n).

The map F is multilinear, hence attains its extrema on V_n.
For a vertex that contains k ones (0\leq k\leq n) we have  

  F = I_{k=n} - \theta  k,

where I_{k=n} is 1 when k=n and 0 otherwise.  Thus  

  \|P-\Sigma g\|\infty  = max_{0\leq k\leq n} |I_{k=n} - \theta k|
       = max{ |1-n\theta | , (n-1)\theta  }.                            (8)

This is formula ().  
If \theta =1/(2n), then (8) gives error max{\frac{1}{2},(n-1)/(2n)} = \frac{1}{2}.

To optimise (8) with respect to \theta , solve  

  1-n\theta  = (n-1)\theta   \Rightarrow   \theta  = 1/(2n-1).                       (9)

Because 1/(2n-1) \leq  1/n, the value  

  \theta *_n = 1/(2n-1)                                               (10)

is admissible, and inserting it into (8) yields  

  E*_n = 1-n\theta *_n = (n-1)/(2n-1) < \frac{1}{2}.                         (11)

Notice that E*_n \uparrow  \frac{1}{2} as n\to \infty .  Statement (11) also shows that
within the symmetric linear family the choice \theta *_n is optimal and beats
the previously used value \theta =1/(2n).

(c)  Limiting behaviour.  

Part 3(a) gives C_n \geq  2^{-n}, whence liminf_{n\to \infty } C_n \geq  0, while Part 3(b)
produces admissible approximations with error E*_n \to  \frac{1}{2}, hence
limsup_{n\to \infty } C_n \leq  \frac{1}{2}.  Whether the limit exists and, if so, whether
it equals \frac{1}{2} are - at the time of writing - completely open problems.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Dimension jump: The original statement concerns two variables; the new version demands handling an arbitrary number n ≥ 3, introducing exponentially many (2ⁿ) corner points.  
• Technical toolkit: The proof employs multi-index notation, parity (-1)^{|ε|}, and a higher-dimensional alternating-sum identity that generalises the “four-corner’’ trick of the original problem; recognising and proving this identity is already a non-trivial combinatorial/algebraic step.  
• Sharper quantitative bound: Not only must one show the existence of a suitable point, one must also identify the exact best constant 2⁻ⁿ and prove its optimality, which requires a second layer of reasoning (a contradiction via the same alternating-sum argument).  
• Scalability: For n variables the naïve search space and the bookkeeping of signs grow exponentially, so a direct enumeration strategy is infeasible; the concise argument presented hinges on exploiting multilinear structure and symmetry rather than brute force.  
• Conceptual depth: The problem now sits at the intersection of combinatorial identities, extremal estimates, and low-rank approximations, demanding a synthesis of ideas beyond the single “choose one of four numbers’’ observation that solves the original.

For real-valued functions g_1,\ldots ,g_n on [0,1] write  

  \|P-(g_1+\cdots +g_n)\|\infty  := sup{ |P(x_1,\ldots ,x_n)-g_1(x_1)-\cdots -g_n(x_n)| : (x_1,\ldots ,x_n)\in [0,1]^n }.

    (b) Consider the one-parameter linear family

        Show that within this family the best constant equals \frac{1}{3} and is attained at \alpha =\frac{1}{3}.

3.  (Higher dimensions - quantitative bounds that are unconditional)

    \theta *_n = 1/(2n-1),

        for which the error in () equals  

    E*_n = (n-1)/(2n-1) < \frac{1}{2},

        and E*_n \nearrow  \frac{1}{2} as n\to \infty .

    (c)  Conclude that  

    limsup_{n\to \infty } C_n \leq  \frac{1}{2},

        whereas Part 3(a) gives  

    liminf_{n\to \infty } C_n \geq  0.

Notation.  Throughout we abbreviate  

  \Sigma g(x_1,\ldots ,x_n) := g_1(x_1)+\cdots +g_n(x_n)  and  V_n := {0,1}^n.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Proof of the universal \frac{1}{4}-gap  

Fix any two coordinates, say the first two, and keep the remaining
coordinates equal to 1.  Put  

  F(t):=f_1(t), G(s):=f_2(s)+\sum _{k=3}^{n}f_k(1)   (0\leq s,t\leq 1).

The classical Putnam result (1963) asserts that there are x_1,x_2\in [0,1] with  

  |x_1x_2 - F(x_1) - G(x_2)| \geq  \frac{1}{4}.                                 (1)

Putting x_3=\cdots =x_n=1 makes P(x_1,\ldots ,x_n)=x_1x_2, and (1) is
precisely (\star ).  Hence Part 1 is proved.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The planar constant - sharp bounds and the best linear ansatz  

(a) Lower bound C_2 \geq  \frac{1}{4}.  

Let g_1,g_2 be arbitrary and write  

  a=g_1(0), b=g_1(1), c=g_2(0), d=g_2(1), \varepsilon :=\|P-(g_1+g_2)\|\infty .

Evaluating the approximation at the four vertices gives  

  |a+c|\leq \varepsilon ,     |b+c|\leq \varepsilon ,     |a+d|\leq \varepsilon ,     |1-b-d|\leq \varepsilon .                (2)

Subtracting the first two inequalities yields |a-b|\leq 2\varepsilon , and
subtracting the first from the third yields |c-d|\leq 2\varepsilon .
Now choose the three vertices (1,1), (1,0), (0,1); from (2) we get  

  |1-b-d|\leq \varepsilon , |b+c|\leq \varepsilon , |a+d|\leq \varepsilon .

Adding these three inequalities and invoking the triangle inequality
gives  

  |1-b-d|+|b+c|+|a+d| \geq  |(1-b-d)+(b+c)+(a+d)| = |1+a+c|.      (3)

Using |a+c|\leq \varepsilon  we have |1+a+c| \geq  1-\varepsilon , hence from (3)  

  1-\varepsilon  \leq  3\varepsilon   \Rightarrow   \varepsilon  \geq  \frac{1}{4}.                                   (4)

Because g_1,g_2 were arbitrary, C_2 \geq  \frac{1}{4}.

(b) Upper bound C_2 \leq  \frac{1}{3} and optimality inside the linear family.

For \alpha \in [0,1] put g_1(x)=\alpha x, g_2(y)=\alpha y and  

  E_\alpha (x,y):=|xy-\alpha (x+y)|.

Because E_\alpha  is bilinear/linear in each variable, its maximum on the unit
square is attained at a vertex; a short inspection shows  

  \|E_\alpha \|\infty  = max{\alpha , |1-2\alpha |}.                                    (5)

The right-hand side is minimised at \alpha =\frac{1}{3} and equals \frac{1}{3} there; whence

strictly larger than \frac{1}{3}, so \frac{1}{3} is the best possible **within the linear
one-parameter family**.  Whether some genuinely non-linear pair
(g_1,g_2) can beat \frac{1}{3} is an interesting open question; so far no
example is known, and \frac{1}{4} \leq  C_2 \leq  \frac{1}{3} is the best unconditional result.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Higher dimensions  

(a) General bounds 2^{-n} \leq  C_n \leq  \frac{1}{2}.

*  Lower bound.  
   For x\in V_n put \chi (x):=(-1)^{x_1+\cdots +x_n}.  Because P(x)=1 if x=(1,\ldots ,1) and
   P(x)=0 otherwise, one has  

  \sum _{x\in V_n} \chi (x)P(x) = \chi (1,\ldots ,1)=(-1)^n.                        (6)

   On the other hand,  

  \sum _{x\in V_n} \chi (x)\Sigma g(x)=
   \sum _{i=1}^{n} (g_i(0)-g_i(1)) \sum _{x\in V_n\{i}} \chi (x)=0,         (7)

   because the inner sum vanishes (there are equally many \pm 1 terms).
   Combining (6) and (7) and bounding every single error by
   E:=\|P-\Sigma g\|\infty  gives  

  1 = |\sum _{x\in V_n} \chi (x)(P-\Sigma g)(x)| \leq  2^n E,

   hence C_n \geq  2^{-n}.

*  Upper bound.  
   Choosing the linear ansatz g_i(x)=x/(2n) gives \Sigma g(x)=
   (x_1+\cdots +x_n)/(2n).  Because P(x)\leq min{x_1,\ldots ,x_n}\leq (x_1+\cdots +x_n)/n, we have  

  0 \leq  P(x)-\Sigma g(x) \leq  (1/n-1/(2n))(x_1+\cdots +x_n) \leq  \frac{1}{2},

   and clearly |P(x)-\Sigma g(x)|\leq \frac{1}{2} when P(x)\leq \Sigma g(x).  Thus C_n \leq  \frac{1}{2}.

(b)  The symmetric linear family g_i(x)=\theta x, 0\leq \theta \leq 1/n.

For such a choice, put  

  F(x_1,\ldots ,x_n)=x_1\cdots x_n - \theta (x_1+\cdots +x_n).

The map F is multilinear, hence attains its extrema on V_n.
For a vertex that contains k ones (0\leq k\leq n) we have  

  F = I_{k=n} - \theta  k,

where I_{k=n} is 1 when k=n and 0 otherwise.  Thus  

  \|P-\Sigma g\|\infty  = max_{0\leq k\leq n} |I_{k=n} - \theta k|
       = max{ |1-n\theta | , (n-1)\theta  }.                            (8)

This is formula ().  
If \theta =1/(2n), then (8) gives error max{\frac{1}{2},(n-1)/(2n)} = \frac{1}{2}.

To optimise (8) with respect to \theta , solve  

  1-n\theta  = (n-1)\theta   \Rightarrow   \theta  = 1/(2n-1).                       (9)

Because 1/(2n-1) \leq  1/n, the value  

  \theta *_n = 1/(2n-1)                                               (10)

is admissible, and inserting it into (8) yields  

  E*_n = 1-n\theta *_n = (n-1)/(2n-1) < \frac{1}{2}.                         (11)

Notice that E*_n \uparrow  \frac{1}{2} as n\to \infty .  Statement (11) also shows that
within the symmetric linear family the choice \theta *_n is optimal and beats
the previously used value \theta =1/(2n).

(c)  Limiting behaviour.  

Part 3(a) gives C_n \geq  2^{-n}, whence liminf_{n\to \infty } C_n \geq  0, while Part 3(b)
produces admissible approximations with error E*_n \to  \frac{1}{2}, hence
limsup_{n\to \infty } C_n \leq  \frac{1}{2}.  Whether the limit exists and, if so, whether
it equals \frac{1}{2} are - at the time of writing - completely open problems.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Dimension jump: The original statement concerns two variables; the new version demands handling an arbitrary number n ≥ 3, introducing exponentially many (2ⁿ) corner points.  
• Technical toolkit: The proof employs multi-index notation, parity (-1)^{|ε|}, and a higher-dimensional alternating-sum identity that generalises the “four-corner’’ trick of the original problem; recognising and proving this identity is already a non-trivial combinatorial/algebraic step.  
• Sharper quantitative bound: Not only must one show the existence of a suitable point, one must also identify the exact best constant 2⁻ⁿ and prove its optimality, which requires a second layer of reasoning (a contradiction via the same alternating-sum argument).  
• Scalability: For n variables the naïve search space and the bookkeeping of signs grow exponentially, so a direct enumeration strategy is infeasible; the concise argument presented hinges on exploiting multilinear structure and symmetry rather than brute force.  
• Conceptual depth: The problem now sits at the intersection of combinatorial identities, extremal estimates, and low-rank approximations, demanding a synthesis of ideas beyond the single “choose one of four numbers’’ observation that solves the original.
